enrichment.short_description: Stalwart is an open-source, all-in-one mail and collaboration server written in Rust that combines JMAP, IMAP4, POP3, SMTP, CalDAV, CardDAV, and WebDAV in a single self-hosted platform.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community edition); Enterprise from about 2 euros per mailbox per year
enrichment.seo_title: Stalwart Mail Server: Open-Source All-in-One Email Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Stalwart is a free, open-source mail and collaboration server combining JMAP, IMAP, POP3, SMTP, CalDAV, CardDAV, and WebDAV. Self-hosted and Rust-powered.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Stalwart Mail Server is an open-source, self-hosted mail and collaboration server built in Rust by Stalwart Labs, founded in 2022 and based in London, United Kingdom. It unifies JMAP, IMAP4rev2, IMAP4rev1, POP3, SMTP, ManageSieve, CalDAV, CardDAV, and WebDAV in a single binary, positioning itself as an all-in-one alternative to running separate mail, calendar, and contacts servers. Core features include built-in DKIM, DMARC, SPF, and ARC support for message authentication, automated DKIM key rotation, ACME-based TLS certificate renewal, integrated DNS management for providers like Cloudflare and Route 53, a web-based admin dashboard, multi-tenant architecture with per-user and per-tenant quotas, and Sieve scripting support for every IANA-registered extension. The Community edition is free and fully open source (available on GitHub), while a paid Enterprise edition adds premium support, advanced management tooling, and licensing priced per mailbox (roughly 2 euros per mailbox per year for smaller deployments, decreasing at higher volumes). Stalwart is aimed at system administrators, privacy-focused organizations, and developers who want to self-host a modern, standards-compliant email stack instead of relying on legacy software like Postfix, Dovecot, or hosted providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Stalwart Mail Server
paragraphs: Stalwart Mail Server is an open-source, self-hosted mail and collaboration platform that consolidates the protocols a modern email deployment needs into a single Rust-based server. Rather than stitching together separate software for SMTP, IMAP, and calendaring, Stalwart bundles JMAP, IMAP4, POP3, SMTP, ManageSieve, CalDAV, CardDAV, and WebDAV support in one codebase.

The project is maintained by Stalwart Labs, a company founded in 2022 and based in London, United Kingdom. It targets administrators and organizations who want full control over their email infrastructure without depending on legacy tools like Postfix and Dovecot, or on third-party hosted inboxes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Stalwart supports modern JMAP clients as well as traditional IMAP and POP3 clients, so it works with both contemporary apps and legacy mail software. Built-in DKIM, DMARC, SPF, and ARC support handle message authentication out of the box, with automated DKIM key rotation on a configurable schedule.

Administration is handled through a web-based dashboard covering account, domain, and configuration management, with real-time monitoring and live tracing for troubleshooting. Multi-tenant architecture allows operators to set per-user and per-tenant storage quotas, and integrated DNS management connects directly to providers such as Cloudflare, Route 53, and Google Cloud DNS for automated record management. ACME-based TLS certificate renewal and full Sieve scripting (including every IANA-registered extension) round out the administrative toolset.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Stalwart is free and open source under its Community edition, which includes the core mail server functionality. An Enterprise edition adds premium features, management tooling, and support, licensed per mailbox at roughly 2 euros per mailbox per year for smaller deployments (25 to 499 mailboxes), with the per-mailbox rate decreasing at higher volumes (down to about 0.89 euros per mailbox at 50,000-plus mailboxes). Sponsors contributing at certain monthly tiers on the project's funding page also receive Enterprise licensing and support access.

features.title: Unified protocol support
features.description: JMAP, IMAP4rev2, IMAP4rev1, POP3, and SMTP are all served from a single Rust binary, reducing the number of separate services an administrator has to run.

features.title: Built-in message authentication
features.description: Native DKIM, DMARC, SPF, and ARC support with automated DKIM key rotation help prevent spoofing and improve deliverability without extra tooling.

features.title: Integrated calendar and contacts
features.description: CalDAV and CardDAV support let Stalwart double as a calendaring and contacts backend alongside email.

features.title: Automated TLS and DNS management
features.description: ACME-based certificate renewal and direct integration with DNS providers like Cloudflare and Route 53 simplify ongoing certificate and record maintenance.

features.title: Multi-tenant architecture
features.description: Per-user and per-tenant quotas make Stalwart suitable for hosting providers or organizations running mail for multiple domains and departments.

features.title: Web-based admin dashboard
features.description: A browser-based control panel manages accounts, domains, and configuration, with live tracing and real-time dashboards for operational visibility.

features.title: Full Sieve scripting
features.description: Support for every IANA-registered Sieve extension gives users granular control over mail filtering rules.

features.title: Open-source and self-hosted
features.description: The Community edition source code is available on GitHub, giving organizations full transparency and control over their email infrastructure.

pros: Consolidates SMTP, IMAP, POP3, JMAP, CalDAV, CardDAV, and WebDAV into one server instead of many separate tools
Free and open source Community edition with an active GitHub project
Modern Rust codebase built for performance and memory safety
Strong built-in email authentication (DKIM, DMARC, SPF, ARC) reduces reliance on add-on tools
Automated DNS and TLS certificate management reduces manual admin overhead
cons: Self-hosting email requires ongoing operational responsibility for deliverability, security, and uptime
Enterprise features and premium support require a paid license
Newer project (founded 2022) with a smaller long-term track record than legacy mail servers like Postfix or Dovecot
Migrating from an existing mail stack requires planning around DNS, DKIM keys, and client reconfiguration

faqs.question: Is Stalwart Mail Server free to use
faqs.answer: Yes. The Community edition is free and open source. A paid Enterprise edition exists for organizations that want premium support and additional management features, billed per mailbox.

faqs.question: What protocols does Stalwart support
faqs.answer: Stalwart supports JMAP, IMAP4rev2, IMAP4rev1, POP3, SMTP, ManageSieve, CalDAV, CardDAV, and WebDAV in a single server.

faqs.question: Is Stalwart self-hosted or a hosted service
faqs.answer: Stalwart is designed to be self-hosted. Organizations run it on their own servers or infrastructure rather than subscribing to a hosted inbox service.

faqs.question: What programming language is Stalwart written in
faqs.answer: Stalwart is written in Rust, which the project cites as contributing to its performance, security, and memory safety.

faqs.question: Who maintains Stalwart Mail Server
faqs.answer: Stalwart is developed by Stalwart Labs, a company founded in 2022 and based in London, United Kingdom, with an active open-source community on GitHub, Discord, and Matrix.

faqs.question: Does Stalwart support DKIM and DMARC out of the box
faqs.answer: Yes. Stalwart includes built-in support for DKIM, DMARC, SPF, and ARC, including automated DKIM key rotation.

faqs.question: Can Stalwart replace Postfix and Dovecot
faqs.answer: Stalwart is designed as an all-in-one replacement for the typical Postfix plus Dovecot combination, handling both SMTP delivery and IMAP or JMAP mailbox access in one server.

faqs.question: Does Stalwart support multiple domains and tenants
faqs.answer: Yes. Stalwart has a multi-tenant architecture with per-user and per-tenant quotas, making it suitable for hosting mail for multiple organizations or domains.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted email for privacy-focused organizations
use_cases.description: Organizations that want full control over their email data and infrastructure can run Stalwart on their own servers instead of using a third-party hosted provider.

use_cases.title: Consolidating mail, calendar, and contacts infrastructure
use_cases.description: Teams that currently run separate SMTP, IMAP, CalDAV, and CardDAV servers can replace them with a single Stalwart deployment.

use_cases.title: Mail hosting for multiple domains or clients
use_cases.description: Stalwart's multi-tenant architecture and per-mailbox Enterprise pricing suit hosting providers or agencies managing email for multiple client domains.
use_cases.audience: Managed hosting providers and web agencies

features: JMAP, IMAP4, POP3, and SMTP support
Built-in DKIM, DMARC, SPF, and ARC

pricing_plans.price: From about 2 euros per mailbox per year (25 to 499 mailboxes), decreasing at higher volumes

enrichment.short_description: Standard Notes is an end-to-end encrypted note-taking app that syncs across all major platforms, built for privacy-conscious individuals and teams.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Productivity plan from about 90 dollars per year)
enrichment.seo_title: Standard Notes: End-to-End Encrypted Note-Taking App
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Standard Notes is an end-to-end encrypted, cross-platform note-taking app with a free tier and paid plans for extended history, files, and themes.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Standard Notes is an end-to-end encrypted note-taking application founded by Mo Bitar in 2016 in Chicago, Illinois, created as a privacy-focused response to policy changes at competitors like Evernote. In April 2024 it was acquired by Proton AG, joining the Proton suite of privacy products alongside Proton Mail and Proton VPN. The app provides zero-knowledge, audited end-to-end encryption so that only the user can decrypt their notes, with sync across web, desktop, and mobile platforms. Standard Notes operates on a freemium model: the free tier includes markdown support, nested folders, and core organization features, while paid plans (roughly 90 dollars per year for the Productivity tier and around 120 dollars per year for the Professional tier) unlock features like extended note history, file storage (up to 100GB on the top tier), custom themes and editors, and subscription sharing. It is used by individuals, journalists, and small teams who prioritize data privacy and want an alternative to cloud note apps that do not encrypt content client-side.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Standard Notes
paragraphs: Standard Notes is an end-to-end encrypted note-taking app designed around a simple premise: notes should be private by default. Every note is encrypted on-device before it ever reaches Standard Notes' servers, so only the account holder holds the keys required to decrypt their data.

The app was founded by Mo Bitar in 2016 in Chicago, Illinois, in direct response to privacy policy changes at mainstream note-taking competitors. In April 2024, Standard Notes was acquired by Proton AG, the company behind Proton Mail and Proton VPN, and now sits alongside those products in Proton's privacy-focused suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Standard Notes offers markdown editing, nested folders and tags for organization, and smart search, all wrapped in audited end-to-end encryption. Notes sync across web, desktop, and mobile apps so the same encrypted library is accessible everywhere.

Paid tiers extend the free experience with longer note version history, larger encrypted file storage allowances, additional themes and specialized editors (such as rich text, code, and task list editors), and the ability to share a subscription with family members or a small team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Standard Notes uses a freemium model. The free plan covers core note-taking, markdown, folders, and encryption for individuals who do not need extended history or extra storage. The Productivity plan, priced at roughly 90 dollars per year, adds extended note history and more editors. The Professional plan, at roughly 120 dollars per year, adds larger encrypted file storage (up to 100GB) and subscription sharing for multiple users.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: All notes and files are encrypted on-device with audited encryption before syncing, so Standard Notes itself cannot read user content.

features.title: Cross-platform sync
features.description: Notes stay in sync across web, Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android apps.

features.title: Markdown and rich editors
features.description: Supports plain markdown as well as optional rich text, code, and task-list editors on paid tiers.

features.title: Nested folders and tags
features.description: Notes can be organized with nested folders, tags, and smart filters for quick retrieval.

features.title: Note version history
features.description: Paid plans retain historical versions of notes so earlier edits can be recovered.

features.title: Encrypted file storage
features.description: Higher tiers include encrypted file attachments with storage allowances up to 100GB.

features.title: Offline access
features.description: Notes remain accessible and editable offline, syncing again once a connection is restored.

features.title: Subscription sharing
features.description: The Professional plan allows a subscription to be shared across multiple people.

pros: Strong, audited end-to-end encryption with a zero-knowledge architecture
Generous free tier covering core note-taking needs
Cross-platform apps for web, desktop, and mobile
Open-source clients allow independent security review
Now backed by Proton's broader privacy infrastructure after the 2024 acquisition
cons: Advanced features like extended history and large file storage require a paid plan
Editor and organization features are more minimal than some mainstream note apps
No built-in real-time collaborative editing comparable to Notion or Google Docs
Pricing and product direction may shift as integration with Proton continues

faqs.question: Is Standard Notes really end-to-end encrypted
faqs.answer: Yes. Standard Notes encrypts notes and files on-device before syncing, using audited end-to-end encryption, so only the account holder can decrypt their data.

faqs.question: Is Standard Notes free
faqs.answer: Yes, Standard Notes has a free tier with core note-taking, markdown, and encryption. Paid Productivity and Professional plans add extended history, more storage, and additional editors.

faqs.question: Who owns Standard Notes now
faqs.answer: Standard Notes was acquired by Proton AG in April 2024 and is now part of Proton's suite of privacy products alongside Proton Mail and Proton VPN.

faqs.question: What platforms does Standard Notes support
faqs.answer: Standard Notes has apps for web, Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android, all syncing the same encrypted note library.

faqs.question: Can I use Standard Notes offline
faqs.answer: Yes, notes are accessible and editable offline and sync automatically once the device reconnects.

faqs.question: Does Standard Notes support markdown
faqs.answer: Yes, markdown is supported natively, and paid plans add additional editor types such as rich text and code editors.

faqs.question: How much does Standard Notes cost
faqs.answer: Standard Notes offers a free plan, a Productivity plan at roughly 90 dollars per year, and a Professional plan at roughly 120 dollars per year.

faqs.question: Who founded Standard Notes
faqs.answer: Standard Notes was founded by Mo Bitar in 2016 in Chicago, Illinois.

use_cases.title: Private personal journaling and notes
use_cases.description: Individuals who want a private place to keep journals, ideas, and sensitive information use Standard Notes' encrypted storage to keep content away from third-party access.

use_cases.title: Secure notes for journalists and researchers
use_cases.description: Professionals handling sensitive sources or research material rely on Standard Notes' zero-knowledge encryption to protect their notes.

use_cases.title: Shared encrypted notes for small teams or families
use_cases.description: The Professional plan's subscription sharing lets small teams or families maintain their own encrypted note libraries under one subscription.

enrichment.short_description: Starlight is a free, open-source documentation site framework built on Astro, offering navigation, search, internationalization, and accessibility out of the box.
enrichment.seo_title: Starlight: Open-Source Documentation Site Builder for Astro
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Starlight is a free, open-source Astro-based framework for building fast, accessible, searchable documentation websites with built-in i18n.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Starlight is a free, open-source documentation site framework built on top of Astro, maintained by the Astro core team (withastro) since its introduction in 2023. It is designed to help developers and technical writers build fast, accessible documentation websites with minimal setup. Starlight ships with automatic sidebar navigation generated from a site's file structure, built-in full-text search powered by Pagefind, built-in internationalization for multilingual documentation, dark and light theme support, and support for Markdown, MDX, and Markdoc content formats. Because it is built on Astro, Starlight ships minimal JavaScript to the browser by default, producing fast page loads, and it supports framework-agnostic UI components so teams can drop in React, Vue, Svelte, or Solid components where needed. Starlight is entirely free and open source, distributed under an open-source license on GitHub, with no paid tier or company behind it beyond the Astro project itself. It suits open-source projects, developer tools, API references, and any team that wants a polished documentation site without building one from scratch.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Starlight
paragraphs: Starlight is a documentation site framework built on Astro, created to give developers a fast path to a polished, accessible documentation website. Rather than building a docs site from scratch, teams configure Starlight and get navigation, search, theming, and internationalization by default.

Starlight is maintained by the Astro core team as an open-source project, first introduced in 2023. It has become a popular choice for open-source project documentation, developer tools, and API references because it emphasizes performance and accessibility without requiring extensive custom development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Starlight automatically generates site navigation from a project's folder and file structure, and includes built-in full-text search powered by Pagefind so visitors can find content without an external search service.

The framework supports Markdown, MDX, and Markdoc for content authoring, includes built-in internationalization for multilingual documentation sites, and ships dark and light theme support along with accessible typography and code syntax highlighting. Because it is built on Astro, Starlight sends minimal JavaScript to the browser, keeping documentation sites fast, and it supports framework-agnostic components so teams can embed React, Vue, Svelte, or Solid UI where interactivity is needed.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Starlight is completely free and open source. There is no paid tier, subscription, or license fee; teams self-host the generated static site wherever they choose, such as Netlify, Vercel, or GitHub Pages.

features.title: Automatic navigation
features.description: Sidebar navigation is generated automatically from a project's folders and files, reducing manual configuration.

features.title: Built-in search
features.description: Full-text search powered by Pagefind is included out of the box with no external search service required.

features.title: Internationalization
features.description: Built-in i18n support makes it straightforward to build and maintain multilingual documentation sites.

features.title: Multiple content formats
features.description: Supports Markdown, MDX, and Markdoc, giving authors flexibility in how they write content.

features.title: Accessible by default
features.description: Ships with accessible typography, semantic navigation, and keyboard-friendly components.

features.title: Dark and light themes
features.description: Includes built-in theme switching between dark and light modes.

features.title: Minimal JavaScript footprint
features.description: Built on Astro's islands architecture, so pages load fast by shipping little client-side JavaScript.

features.title: Framework-agnostic components
features.description: Supports embedding React, Vue, Svelte, Solid, and other framework components inside documentation pages.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tier
Fast page loads thanks to Astro's minimal-JavaScript approach
Built-in search, navigation, and i18n reduce setup time
Actively maintained by the Astro core team with frequent releases
Flexible content authoring across Markdown, MDX, and Markdoc
cons: Requires familiarity with Astro and its build tooling
No hosted or managed offering; teams must deploy and host it themselves
Customization beyond the built-in theme requires editing Astro components
Smaller plugin ecosystem than long-established docs tools like Docusaurus or GitBook

faqs.question: Is Starlight free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Starlight is completely free and open source with no paid tiers or licensing fees.

faqs.question: What is Starlight built on
faqs.answer: Starlight is built on Astro, a web framework focused on shipping minimal JavaScript for fast page loads.

faqs.question: Does Starlight include search
faqs.answer: Yes, Starlight includes built-in full-text search powered by Pagefind, requiring no external search service.

faqs.question: Can Starlight sites support multiple languages
faqs.answer: Yes, Starlight has built-in internationalization support for building multilingual documentation sites.

faqs.question: What content formats does Starlight support
faqs.answer: Starlight supports Markdown, MDX, and Markdoc for writing documentation content.

faqs.question: Who maintains Starlight
faqs.answer: Starlight is maintained by the Astro core team as part of the open-source Astro ecosystem, first introduced in 2023.

faqs.question: Can I use React or Vue components in Starlight
faqs.answer: Yes, Starlight supports framework-agnostic UI components, so React, Vue, Svelte, and Solid components can be embedded in documentation pages.

faqs.question: Where can I host a Starlight documentation site
faqs.answer: Since Starlight generates a static Astro site, it can be hosted anywhere static sites are supported, including Netlify, Vercel, and GitHub Pages.

use_cases.title: Open-source project documentation
use_cases.description: Open-source maintainers use Starlight to build searchable, well-organized docs for their libraries and tools without building a custom site.

use_cases.title: Developer tool and API documentation
use_cases.description: Companies building developer tools use Starlight to document APIs and SDKs with fast, accessible, search-enabled sites.

use_cases.title: Multilingual product documentation
use_cases.description: Teams needing documentation in multiple languages use Starlight's built-in internationalization to manage translated content in one site.

features: Community support via GitHub and Discord

enrichment.short_description: Stash (stashapp.cc) is a free, open-source, self-hosted media organizer written in Go for cataloging, tagging, and browsing personal video and image collections.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Stash is a free, open-source, self-hosted media organizer written in Go with tagging, metadata scraping, statistics, and plugin support.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Stash, distributed from stashapp.cc and developed in the stashapp GitHub organization, is a free, open-source, self-hosted web application for organizing and browsing personal media libraries. It is not related to the Stash investing or personal finance app; this Stash is a media cataloging tool written primarily in Go with a TypeScript front end, licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 (AGPL-3.0). Users run Stash on their own hardware, typically via Docker, on Windows, macOS, or Linux, and access it through a local web interface (by default at localhost:9999). Core capabilities include tagging and organizing video and image files, pulling metadata from the internet through an extensible community-built scraper and plugin system, tracking statistics on performers, tags, and studios, and supporting a wide range of video and image formats (with ffmpeg used for transcoding). The project also maintains StashDB, a crowd-sourced metadata repository that scrapers can pull from. Stash has an active open-source community with documentation at docs.stashapp.cc, a Discord server, GitHub discussions, and roughly 12,700 GitHub stars. It is aimed at users who want full local control over organizing large personal media collections rather than relying on cloud services.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Stash
paragraphs: Stash is a free, open-source, self-hosted web application for organizing personal media collections. It runs on the user's own hardware rather than in the cloud, giving full control over how files are stored, tagged, and browsed.

The project is maintained by the stashapp open-source community and distributed at stashapp.cc. It is written primarily in Go with a TypeScript front end, and is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0. It should not be confused with the unrelated Stash investing and personal finance app; stashapp.cc is strictly a self-hosted media organizer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Stash lets users tag, categorize, and search their media library, and gathers metadata automatically through an extensible plugin and scraper system built by the community. It tracks statistics across performers, tags, and studios, and integrates with StashDB, a crowd-sourced metadata repository that scrapers can pull structured information from.

The application supports a wide range of video and image formats and uses ffmpeg for transcoding (downloading it automatically if not already installed). It runs via Docker or as a native application on Windows, macOS, and Linux, and is accessed through a local web interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Stash is entirely free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license. There is no paid tier from the core project; the community also supports the project through voluntary contributions via Open Collective. Some third-party providers offer paid managed hosting for Stash, but the software itself is free to self-host.

features.title: Media tagging and organization
features.description: Videos and images can be tagged, categorized, and searched within a personal media library.

features.title: Automated metadata scraping
features.description: Community-built scrapers pull metadata such as titles, performers, and studios from external sources.

features.title: Plugin extensibility
features.description: An extensible plugin system lets the community add support for new sources and functionality.

features.title: Performer, tag, and studio statistics
features.description: Stash tracks and displays statistics across performers, tags, and studios in the library.

features.title: StashDB integration
features.description: Connects to StashDB, a crowd-sourced metadata repository, for enriched scene and performer information.

features.title: Broad format support
features.description: Handles a wide variety of video and image formats, using ffmpeg for transcoding when needed.

features.title: Cross-platform self-hosting
features.description: Runs via Docker or natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

features.title: Local web interface
features.description: Managed through a browser-based interface hosted locally, by default at localhost:9999.

pros: Completely free and open source under AGPL-3.0
Full local control over media library and metadata, no cloud dependency
Active community with extensive plugin and scraper ecosystem
Cross-platform support via Docker, Windows, macOS, and Linux
Detailed statistics and organization tools for large collections
cons: Requires self-hosting and basic technical setup (Docker or manual install)
No official cloud-hosted version from the core project
Metadata scraping quality depends on community-maintained scrapers
Not related to and easily confused with the Stash personal finance investing app

faqs.question: Is Stash the same as the Stash investing app
faqs.answer: No. Stash (stashapp.cc) is an open-source, self-hosted media organizer written in Go. It is unrelated to the Stash personal finance and investing app.

faqs.question: Is Stash free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Stash is free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license, with no official paid tier.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Stash support
faqs.answer: Stash runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux, and is commonly deployed via Docker.

faqs.question: How do I access Stash once it is running
faqs.answer: Stash is accessed through a local web interface, available by default at localhost:9999.

faqs.question: Does Stash require ffmpeg
faqs.answer: Yes, Stash requires ffmpeg for transcoding and will download a copy automatically if it is not already installed.

faqs.question: What is StashDB
faqs.answer: StashDB is a crowd-sourced repository of scene, studio, and performer metadata that Stash's scrapers can use to enrich a user's library.

faqs.question: What programming language is Stash written in
faqs.answer: Stash is written primarily in Go, with a TypeScript front end.

faqs.question: Where can I get support for Stash
faqs.answer: Support is available through the official documentation at docs.stashapp.cc, a Discord server, and GitHub discussions.

use_cases.title: Personal media library organization
use_cases.description: Individuals with large personal video and image collections use Stash to tag, catalog, and search their files from a local web interface.
use_cases.audience: Self-hosting enthusiasts and home media curators

use_cases.title: Metadata enrichment for existing collections
use_cases.description: Users with unorganized media libraries use Stash's scraper and plugin system to automatically pull in titles, performers, and studio metadata.
use_cases.audience: Users with large unorganized media archives

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused local media management
use_cases.description: Users who prefer to avoid cloud storage for personal media rely on Stash's fully self-hosted architecture to keep data on their own hardware.

features: Community support via Discord and GitHub

enrichment.short_description: Statamic is a flat-file-first, hybrid CMS built on Laravel that lets developers manage content with Git-friendly files or a database, paired with a polished control panel for editors.
enrichment.pricing_model: freemium (free Core tier, paid Pro licenses)
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Core); Pro from $349 per site first year, $99/year renewal
enrichment.seo_title: Statamic: Flat-File Laravel CMS for Developers and Editors
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Statamic is a Laravel-powered, flat-file-first CMS with a free Core tier and Pro licensing from $349 per site, built for developers and content editors.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Statamic is a content management system built on the Laravel PHP framework, founded in 2012 by Jack McDade (with early co-founder Mubashar Iqbal) and headquartered in Ormond Beach, Florida. It is a small, self-funded, privately held company with roughly 2 to 10 employees, with Jason Varga later joining as a core developer. Statamic is known for being flat-file-first, meaning content can be stored as version-controllable files (Markdown, YAML) rather than requiring a database, while also supporting a database-driven mode for teams that prefer it. It targets developers who want Git-based workflows and full code control, paired with a polished control panel that non-technical editors can use to manage content. Pricing follows a freemium model: the Core tier is free forever for single-admin sites, while the Pro tier costs 349 dollars per site for the first year (99 dollars per year to renew) and unlocks unlimited users and roles, revisions and content history, headless REST and GraphQL APIs, multi-site and multilingual support, and white-label customization. An Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with SSO, audit logging, and dedicated support. Statamic is popular for marketing sites, blogs, documentation sites, and client projects built by Laravel developers and agencies.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Statamic
paragraphs: Statamic is a content management system built on top of Laravel, designed around the idea that content can live in flat files rather than being locked inside a database. Founded in 2012 by Jack McDade, Statamic set out to give developers Git-friendly, version-controllable content while still offering a polished editing experience for non-technical users.

The company is headquartered in Ormond Beach, Florida, and remains a small, self-funded team of roughly 2 to 10 people. Core developer Jason Varga joined the project after building an early e-commerce add-on, and the two have driven Statamic's development since.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Statamic's flat-file-first approach lets content, structured through Blueprints and more than 40 field types, be stored as Markdown and YAML files that can be tracked in Git alongside code, while also supporting a database driver for teams that prefer it. The control panel gives editors a clean, modern interface for managing collections, taxonomies, and assets without touching code.

Pro-tier features add unlimited user accounts and roles, content revisions and version history, a headless REST and GraphQL API for decoupled front ends, multi-site and multilingual publishing, and white-label customization for agencies building on behalf of clients. Git integration and automation tools in the control panel support CI/CD-style content workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Statamic Core is free forever for single-admin use, and Pro is free during development and staging. For production use, the Pro license costs 349 dollars per site for the first year, renewing at 99 dollars per year thereafter, and unlocks multi-user editing, the headless API, and other production features most client projects need. An Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with SSO, two-factor authentication, audit logging, and dedicated premium support with an SLA. Verified nonprofits, schools, and universities receive a 20 percent discount on first-party products.

features.title: Flat-file-first content storage
features.description: Content can be stored as Markdown and YAML files that live alongside code and are trackable in Git, with an optional database driver.

features.title: Blueprints and field types
features.description: Content structures are defined with Blueprints using more than 40 built-in field types for flexible content modeling.

features.title: Headless REST and GraphQL API
features.description: Pro-tier sites can serve content headlessly through REST or GraphQL for decoupled front ends.

features.title: Multi-site and multilingual support
features.description: A single Statamic installation can power multiple sites and languages from one control panel.

features.title: Git-based workflows
features.description: Git integration and automation in the control panel support developer workflows that track content changes like code.

features.title: Live Preview
features.description: Editors can preview content changes in real time before publishing.

features.title: Roles and permissions
features.description: Pro tier supports unlimited user accounts with configurable roles and permissions for editorial teams.

features.title: White-label customization
features.description: Agencies can rebrand the control panel for client-facing deployments on the Pro tier.

pros: Free Core tier with no cost for single-admin, single-site use
Flat-file storage enables true Git-based content version control
Built on Laravel, appealing to PHP and Laravel developers
Polished, modern control panel that non-technical editors can use comfortably
Flexible headless API options for decoupled front-end architectures
cons: Pro tier requires a per-site license fee, which can add up for agencies managing many sites
Smaller plugin and theme ecosystem compared to WordPress
Requires PHP and Laravel familiarity to extend or customize deeply
Flat-file storage can become harder to manage at very large content scale

faqs.question: Is Statamic free to use
faqs.answer: Statamic Core is free forever for single-admin use. The Pro tier, needed for most production client sites, costs 349 dollars per site for the first year and 99 dollars per year to renew.

faqs.question: What framework is Statamic built on
faqs.answer: Statamic is built on Laravel, a popular PHP web application framework.

faqs.question: Does Statamic require a database
faqs.answer: No. Statamic is flat-file-first, storing content as Markdown and YAML files, though it also supports a database driver for teams that prefer that approach.

faqs.question: Who founded Statamic
faqs.answer: Statamic was founded in 2012 by Jack McDade, with Jason Varga later joining as a core developer.

faqs.question: Can Statamic be used as a headless CMS
faqs.answer: Yes, the Pro tier includes a headless REST API and GraphQL API for decoupled front-end architectures.

faqs.question: Does Statamic support multiple languages and sites
faqs.answer: Yes, Statamic Pro supports multi-site and multilingual publishing from a single installation.

faqs.question: Where is Statamic based
faqs.answer: Statamic is headquartered in Ormond Beach, Florida, United States, and operates as a small, self-funded team.

faqs.question: Is there a discount for nonprofits or schools
faqs.answer: Yes, verified nonprofits, schools, and universities receive a 20 percent discount on Statamic's first-party products.

use_cases.title: Agency client websites
use_cases.description: Web agencies use Statamic's white-label Pro tier and Git-based workflows to build and maintain marketing sites for multiple clients.

use_cases.title: Developer-controlled content sites
use_cases.description: Laravel developers use Statamic's flat-file storage to keep content under version control alongside application code.

use_cases.title: Headless content delivery
use_cases.description: Teams building decoupled front ends use Statamic Pro's REST and GraphQL APIs to serve content to custom applications.

features: SSO, two-factor authentication, and audit logging

enrichment.short_description: Statsig is a product development platform combining feature flags, A/B testing and experimentation, and product analytics in a single system built for engineering-led growth.
enrichment.seo_title: Statsig: Feature Flags, Experimentation and Product Analytics
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Statsig unifies feature flags, A/B testing, and product analytics. Free Developer tier, Pro from $150/month, acquired by OpenAI in 2025.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Statsig is a product development platform that unifies feature flags, A/B testing and experimentation, session replay, and product analytics in a single system. It was founded in 2021 by Vijaye Raji, a former Facebook engineering leader, and is headquartered in Bellevue, Washington, with roughly 51 to 200 employees. Statsig raised a Series A led by Sequoia Capital in 2021, a Series B led by Sequoia in 2022, and a 100 million dollar Series C led by ICONIQ Growth in May 2025 at a 1.1 billion dollar valuation, for total funding of about 153.4 million dollars. In September 2025, Statsig was acquired by OpenAI in an all-stock deal valued at 1.1 billion dollars, with plans to continue operating its platform for external customers. Statsig's pricing includes a free Developer tier with 2 million monthly events, unlimited feature flag checks, and 50,000 monthly session replays; a Pro tier at 150 dollars per month with 5 million included events, advanced experimentation, and unlimited analytics retention; and a custom-priced Enterprise tier with warehouse-native deployment, SSO, and HIPAA eligibility. Statsig is used by engineering and product teams at companies of all sizes to ship features safely, run experiments, and analyze product usage without stitching together separate tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Statsig
paragraphs: Statsig is a product development platform that combines feature flagging, A/B testing and experimentation, session replay, and product analytics into a single system, aimed at engineering and product teams that want to ship and measure changes without assembling multiple point tools.

The company was founded in 2021 by Vijaye Raji, previously an engineering leader at Facebook, and is headquartered in Bellevue, Washington. Statsig grew quickly, raising a Series A and Series B led by Sequoia Capital before a 100 million dollar Series C led by ICONIQ Growth in May 2025 at a 1.1 billion dollar valuation. In September 2025, OpenAI acquired Statsig in an all-stock deal also valued at 1.1 billion dollars.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Statsig provides unlimited feature flag and config checks, letting engineering teams roll features out gradually and roll them back instantly if something goes wrong. Its experimentation engine runs A/B tests and more advanced experiment designs with statistical rigor built in.

Product and web analytics are integrated directly with experimentation data, so teams can see the downstream impact of a feature flag or experiment on real usage metrics. Session replay capabilities let teams watch how users actually interact with new features, and higher tiers add change reviews and approvals, API controls, warehouse-native deployment, and SSO for larger organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Statsig's Developer tier is free, with no credit card required, and includes 2 million monthly events, unlimited feature flag checks, 50,000 monthly session replays, and unlimited seats. The Pro tier costs 150 dollars per month, includes 5 million events with additional events billed at 0.05 dollars per 1,000, and adds advanced experimentation, advanced analytics, unlimited analytics retention, and change reviews. The Enterprise tier uses custom, volume-based contracts and adds warehouse-native deployment, data integrations, SSO, role-based access control, and HIPAA eligibility.

features.title: Feature flags and config management
features.description: Unlimited feature flag and configuration checks let teams control feature rollouts without redeploying code.

features.title: A/B testing and experimentation
features.description: Built-in statistical engine supports running and analyzing A/B tests and more advanced experiment designs.

features.title: Product and web analytics
features.description: Usage analytics are integrated directly with flag and experiment data for unified reporting.

features.title: Session replay
features.description: Teams can watch recorded user sessions to see how real users interact with new features.

features.title: Change reviews and approvals
features.description: Pro and Enterprise tiers add workflow controls so flag and experiment changes can be reviewed before going live.

features.title: Warehouse-native deployment
features.description: Enterprise customers can run Statsig against their own data warehouse for data residency and integration needs.

features.title: SSO and role-based access control
features.description: Enterprise tier adds single sign-on and granular access controls for larger organizations.

features.title: Unlimited seats on the free tier
features.description: The Developer plan includes unlimited seats, letting whole teams collaborate without per-seat costs.

pros: Generous free Developer tier with unlimited flag checks and unlimited seats
Combines feature flags, experimentation, and analytics in one platform instead of separate tools
Backed by rapid growth and, since 2025, OpenAI's resources following its acquisition
Advanced experimentation and analytics on paid tiers support rigorous statistical testing
Session replay is included even on the free tier
cons: Event-based pricing on Pro can grow with product usage, adding cost as event volume increases
Enterprise-grade capabilities like warehouse-native deployment and SSO require a custom contract
Recent OpenAI acquisition (2025) may bring product direction changes for existing customers
Advanced analytics retention and change review workflows are gated behind paid tiers

faqs.question: Is Statsig free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Statsig has a free Developer tier with 2 million monthly events, unlimited feature flag checks, and 50,000 monthly session replays.

faqs.question: Who founded Statsig and when
faqs.answer: Statsig was founded in 2021 by Vijaye Raji, a former Facebook engineering leader.

faqs.question: Where is Statsig headquartered
faqs.answer: Statsig is headquartered in Bellevue, Washington, United States.

faqs.question: Did OpenAI acquire Statsig
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenAI acquired Statsig in September 2025 in an all-stock deal valued at 1.1 billion dollars.

faqs.question: How much does Statsig Pro cost
faqs.answer: The Pro tier costs 150 dollars per month, including 5 million events, with additional events billed at 0.05 dollars per 1,000.

faqs.question: What does Statsig's platform include
faqs.answer: Statsig combines feature flags and configuration management, A/B testing and experimentation, session replay, and product analytics in one platform.

faqs.question: Does Statsig offer an Enterprise plan
faqs.answer: Yes, Statsig's Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with warehouse-native deployment, SSO, role-based access control, and HIPAA eligibility.

faqs.question: How much funding did Statsig raise before being acquired
faqs.answer: Statsig raised about 153.4 million dollars across a Series A, Series B, and Series C, reaching a 1.1 billion dollar valuation before OpenAI's acquisition.

use_cases.title: Safe feature rollouts
use_cases.description: Engineering teams use Statsig's feature flags to gradually roll out new features and instantly roll them back if issues arise.

use_cases.title: Product experimentation and A/B testing
use_cases.description: Product teams run A/B tests and more advanced experiments through Statsig to measure the impact of changes before a full rollout.

use_cases.title: Unified product analytics and experiment impact analysis
use_cases.description: Teams use Statsig's integrated analytics to see how feature flags and experiments affect real product usage metrics in one place.

features: Unlimited feature flag and config checks

enrichment.short_description: Statusbrew is a social media management platform that lets businesses and agencies publish, monitor, and analyze content across multiple social profiles from one shared inbox.
enrichment.seo_title: Statusbrew: Social Media Management for Teams and Agencies
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Statusbrew is a social media management platform for scheduling, inbox management, and reporting across profiles. Plans start at $89/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Statusbrew is a social media management platform founded in 2011, built to help small and midsize businesses, agencies, and enterprises manage social profiles, publish content, and unify social inboxes from one dashboard. The company has around 11 to 50 employees working across multiple continents, with operational roots in Amritsar, Punjab, India, and a US corporate registration in Wilmington, Delaware. Statusbrew positions itself as offering close feature parity with larger competitors like Sprout Social and Hootsuite at a lower price. Its plans include Lite at 89 dollars per month for 1 user and 5 social profiles, Standard at 179 dollars per month for 3 users and 10 profiles, Premium at 299 dollars per month for 6 users and 15 profiles (with AI sentiment analysis and approval workflows), and a custom-priced Enterprise tier with dedicated account management, CRM integrations, and SSO. An Agency plan starts at 49 dollars per month per client. All paid plans are billed monthly or annually and come with a 14-day free trial requiring no credit card. Core capabilities include scheduled and bulk publishing, a shared social inbox for managing comments and messages, social media reporting, Google Business Profile integration, and, on higher tiers, competitor benchmarking and social listening.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Statusbrew
paragraphs: Statusbrew is a social media management platform that helps businesses, agencies, and enterprises publish content, manage social inboxes, and report on performance across multiple social profiles from a single dashboard. Founded in 2011, the company has grown into a team of roughly 11 to 50 people working across several continents.

Statusbrew positions itself as a lower-cost alternative to larger platforms like Sprout Social and Hootsuite, aiming for close feature parity while undercutting them on price. It serves customers ranging from small businesses managing a handful of social profiles to agencies running social media for many clients at once.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform supports scheduled and bulk publishing across social channels, with a best-time-to-post recommendation engine to help maximize reach. An all-in-one social inbox consolidates comments, messages, and mentions from every connected profile into a single stream, with a rule engine to automate responses and routing.

Higher tiers add BrewLink-in-bio pages, social media reporting with up to 18 months of historical data, Google Business Profile integration, AI-driven sentiment analysis, assignment and approval workflows for content review, competitor benchmarking, and social and web listening. Enterprise customers get CRM integrations with HubSpot and Salesforce, SAML SSO, API access, and a dedicated account manager.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Statusbrew's Lite plan starts at 89 dollars per month for 1 user and 5 social profiles. Standard is 179 dollars per month for 3 users and 10 profiles, and Premium is 299 dollars per month for up to 6 users and 15 profiles, adding AI sentiment analysis and approval workflows. Enterprise pricing is custom and includes unlimited users and profiles. Agencies can also choose a per-client Agency plan starting at 49 dollars per month per client. All plans include a 14-day free trial with no credit card required.

features.title: Scheduled and bulk publishing
features.description: Content can be scheduled individually or in bulk across multiple social profiles, with best-time-to-post recommendations.

features.title: Unified social inbox
features.description: Comments, messages, and mentions from all connected profiles are consolidated into a single inbox with a rule engine for automation.

features.title: Social media reporting
features.description: Built-in reporting covers performance metrics with up to 18 months of historical data on higher tiers.

features.title: Google Business Profile integration
features.description: Businesses can manage their Google Business Profile listings alongside their other social channels.

features.title: AI sentiment analysis
features.description: Premium and higher tiers use AI to gauge the sentiment of incoming comments and messages.

features.title: Approval workflows
features.description: Content assignment and approval workflows let teams route posts for review before publishing.

features.title: Competitor benchmarking and listening
features.description: Premium and Enterprise tiers include competitor benchmarking and social or web listening tools.

features.title: CRM and enterprise integrations
features.description: Enterprise plans connect to HubSpot and Salesforce, and support SAML SSO and API access.

pros: Positioned as a lower-cost alternative to Sprout Social and Hootsuite with similar feature depth
Unified social inbox simplifies managing comments and messages across many profiles
Dedicated Agency plan with per-client pricing suits agencies managing multiple accounts
14-day free trial with no credit card required
AI sentiment analysis and listening tools available on Premium and above
cons: Entry-level Lite plan supports only 1 user and 5 profiles, limiting it for larger teams
Advanced features like sentiment analysis and competitor benchmarking require the Premium tier or higher
Enterprise-grade integrations (CRM, SSO) require custom pricing and sales contact
Pricing is higher than some lightweight scheduling-only tools aimed at solo creators

faqs.question: How much does Statusbrew cost
faqs.answer: Statusbrew's Lite plan starts at 89 dollars per month for 1 user and 5 social profiles, with Standard at 179 dollars per month and Premium at 299 dollars per month for larger teams.

faqs.question: Does Statusbrew offer a free trial
faqs.answer: Yes, Statusbrew offers a 14-day free trial with no credit card required.

faqs.question: When was Statusbrew founded
faqs.answer: Statusbrew was founded in 2011.

faqs.question: Does Statusbrew have a plan for agencies
faqs.answer: Yes, Statusbrew offers an Agency plan starting at 49 dollars per month per client, with unlimited spaces, calendars, and reports.

faqs.question: What social inbox features does Statusbrew provide
faqs.answer: Statusbrew consolidates comments, messages, and mentions from all connected social profiles into one inbox, with a rule engine to automate routing and responses.

faqs.question: Does Statusbrew integrate with Google Business Profile
faqs.answer: Yes, Statusbrew's Standard tier and above include Google Business Profile integration.

faqs.question: Does Statusbrew support enterprise SSO
faqs.answer: Yes, the Enterprise plan includes SAML SSO along with CRM integrations and dedicated account management.

faqs.question: Who is Statusbrew designed for
faqs.answer: Statusbrew serves small and midsize businesses, agencies, and enterprises that need to manage multiple social media profiles from one platform.

use_cases.title: Multi-profile social media publishing
use_cases.description: Marketing teams use Statusbrew to schedule and bulk-publish content across many social profiles from one calendar.

use_cases.title: Unified customer engagement across social channels
use_cases.description: Support and community teams use Statusbrew's shared inbox to respond to comments and messages from all social profiles in one place.
use_cases.audience: Social media and customer support teams

use_cases.title: Agency management of multiple client accounts
use_cases.description: Agencies use Statusbrew's per-client Agency plan to manage social media publishing and reporting for many clients under one platform.

features: Social media reporting (18 months of data)

enrichment.short_description: StatusCake is a website monitoring platform offering uptime, page speed, SSL, domain, and server monitoring from a global network of testing locations.
enrichment.seo_title: StatusCake: Website Uptime and Performance Monitoring
enrichment.seo_meta_description: StatusCake monitors website uptime, page speed, SSL, and domains from 30-plus global locations. Free tier available; paid plans from about $20/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: StatusCake is a website monitoring platform founded in 2012 by James Barnes and headquartered in London, United Kingdom, operating as a small, privately held company. It monitors uptime, page speed, SSL certificates, domain expiration, and server performance from a network of more than 30 global testing locations, with check intervals as fast as 30 seconds on higher tiers. StatusCake offers a free plan covering 10 uptime monitors with 5-minute test intervals, one page speed monitor, one domain monitor, and one SSL monitor. The Superior plan, priced from about 20.41 dollars per month billed annually (19.99 euros monthly), raises limits to 100 uptime monitors with 1-minute intervals, 15 page speed monitors, 50 domain monitors, 50 SSL monitors, and 3 server monitors. The Business plan, from about 66.66 dollars per month billed annually, is the most popular tier and supports 300 uptime monitors at 30-second intervals, 30 page speed monitors, 120 domain monitors, 100 SSL monitors, and 10 server monitors, along with support for larger teams. A custom-priced Enterprise plan adds unlimited monitors, constant check rates, dedicated monitoring locations, and service-credit-backed SLAs. StatusCake is used by developers, DevOps teams, and small-to-midsize businesses that need to detect downtime and performance issues before customers do.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is StatusCake
paragraphs: StatusCake is a website monitoring platform that tracks uptime, page speed, SSL certificate validity, domain expiration, and server performance to alert teams before issues affect visitors. Founded in 2012 by James Barnes, the company is headquartered in London, United Kingdom, and remains a small, privately held team.

The platform checks websites from more than 30 global locations, letting teams verify availability and performance from the regions where their users actually are, rather than relying on a single test location.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Uptime monitoring runs at intervals as fast as 30 seconds on higher tiers, with alerts sent through email, SMS, and integrations when a site goes down. Page speed monitoring tracks how load times change over time, while SSL and domain monitors flag certificates and domains approaching expiration before they lapse.

Server monitoring, available on paid tiers, tracks resource usage like CPU, memory, and disk on the underlying infrastructure. StatusCake also provides incident communication tools, including public status pages, so teams can keep users informed during outages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: StatusCake offers a free plan with 10 uptime monitors, 5-minute test intervals, and one each of page speed, domain, and SSL monitors. The Superior plan starts from about 20.41 dollars per month billed annually and raises limits to 100 uptime monitors, 1-minute intervals, and more page speed, domain, and SSL monitors, plus 3 server monitors. The Business plan, StatusCake's most popular tier, starts from about 66.66 dollars per month billed annually, offering 300 uptime monitors at 30-second intervals and higher limits across all monitor types. An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with unlimited monitors and dedicated monitoring locations.

features.title: Uptime monitoring
features.description: Checks website availability at intervals as fast as 30 seconds from a global network of testing locations.

features.title: Page speed monitoring
features.description: Tracks page load time trends over time to catch performance regressions.

features.title: SSL certificate monitoring
features.description: Alerts teams before SSL certificates expire to avoid browser security warnings.

features.title: Domain expiration monitoring
features.description: Tracks domain registration expiration dates and warns before a domain lapses.

features.title: Server monitoring
features.description: Available on paid tiers, tracks CPU, memory, and disk usage on underlying servers.

features.title: Global testing locations
features.description: Checks run from more than 30 locations worldwide to reflect real user experience by region.

features.title: Public status pages
features.description: Incident communication tools let teams publish status pages to keep users informed during outages.

features.title: Multi-channel alerting
features.description: Alerts can be delivered via email, SMS, and third-party integrations when issues are detected.

pros: Free tier covers basic uptime, page speed, domain, and SSL monitoring at no cost
Global network of 30-plus testing locations for realistic monitoring by region
Test intervals as fast as 30 seconds on the Business plan
Broad monitoring coverage in one platform (uptime, speed, SSL, domain, server)
Established provider founded in 2012 with a long operating history
cons: Free tier's 5-minute test interval is slower than paid tiers
Server monitoring is only available starting on paid plans
Small team size (2-10 employees) relative to some enterprise monitoring competitors
Enterprise-level features like constant check rates and dedicated locations require custom pricing

faqs.question: Does StatusCake have a free plan
faqs.answer: Yes, StatusCake offers a free plan with 10 uptime monitors, 5-minute test intervals, and one each of page speed, domain, and SSL monitors.

faqs.question: How much does StatusCake cost
faqs.answer: Paid plans start with the Superior plan from about 20.41 dollars per month billed annually, and the Business plan from about 66.66 dollars per month billed annually. Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: How often does StatusCake check my website
faqs.answer: Check intervals range from 5 minutes on the free plan down to 30 seconds on the Business plan.

faqs.question: When was StatusCake founded
faqs.answer: StatusCake was founded in 2012 by James Barnes and is headquartered in London, United Kingdom.

faqs.question: Does StatusCake monitor SSL certificates and domains
faqs.answer: Yes, StatusCake includes SSL certificate monitoring and domain expiration monitoring on all plans, with higher limits on paid tiers.

faqs.question: Does StatusCake support server monitoring
faqs.answer: Yes, server monitoring for CPU, memory, and disk usage is available starting on the Superior paid plan.

faqs.question: How many locations does StatusCake test from
faqs.answer: StatusCake tests from more than 30 global locations to reflect performance and availability by region.

faqs.question: Does StatusCake offer public status pages
faqs.answer: Yes, StatusCake includes incident communication tools, including public status pages, to keep users informed during outages.

use_cases.title: Website uptime alerting
use_cases.description: Businesses use StatusCake to get alerted the moment their website or application goes down, before customers notice.

use_cases.title: DevOps performance and infrastructure monitoring
use_cases.description: DevOps teams use StatusCake's page speed and server monitoring to catch performance regressions and resource issues early.

use_cases.title: SSL and domain expiration tracking
use_cases.description: IT teams use StatusCake to avoid unexpected outages caused by expired SSL certificates or lapsed domain registrations.

features: Dedicated uptime and page speed locations
